title: Ubaldo Arata

(23 March 1895 – 7 December 1947) was an Italian [cinematographer](/wiki/Cinematographer "Cinematographer"). Arata worked on more than a hundred films between 1918 and his death in 1947. Arata entered cinema in the [silent era](/wiki/Silent_era "Silent era") and worked prolifically during the 1920s including on one of the final entries into the long running [Maciste](/wiki/Maciste "Maciste") series. He was employed on the first Italian [sound film](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film") *[The Song of Love](/wiki/The_Song_of_Love_(1930_film) "The Song of Love (1930 film)")* (1930). Until the [fall of Fascism](/wiki/Fall_of_Fascism "Fall of Fascism"), he was one of the leading Italian cinematographers working on [propaganda films](/wiki/Propaganda_of_Fascist_Italy "Propaganda of Fascist Italy") such as *[Scipione l'africano](/wiki/Scipione_l%27africano "Scipione l'africano")* (*Scipio Africanus: The Defeat of Hannibal*, 1937) and *[Luciano Serra, Pilot](/wiki/Luciano_Serra,_Pilot "Luciano Serra, Pilot")* (1938) as well as more straightforward entertainment films.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Italian cinematographer

| Ubaldo Arata | |
| --- | --- |
| Ubaldo Arata, at left, with colleague [Romolo Garroni](/w/index.php?title=Romolo_Garroni&action=edit&redlink=1 "Romolo Garroni (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romolo_Garroni "it:Romolo Garroni")], 1942 | |
| Born | (1895-03-23)23 March 1895  [Ovada](/wiki/Ovada "Ovada"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") |
| Died | 7 December 1947(1947-12-07) (aged 52)  [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), Italy |
| Occupation | Cinematographer |
| Years active | 1918–1947 |

**Ubaldo Arata** (23 March 1895 – 7 December 1947) was an Italian [cinematographer](/wiki/Cinematographer "Cinematographer"). Arata worked on more than a hundred films between 1918 and his death in 1947. Arata entered cinema in the [silent era](/wiki/Silent_era "Silent era") and worked prolifically during the 1920s including on one of the final entries into the long running [Maciste](/wiki/Maciste "Maciste") series. He was employed on the first Italian [sound film](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film") *[The Song of Love](/wiki/The_Song_of_Love_(1930_film) "The Song of Love (1930 film)")* (1930). Until the [fall of Fascism](/wiki/Fall_of_Fascism "Fall of Fascism"), he was one of the leading Italian cinematographers working on [propaganda films](/wiki/Propaganda_of_Fascist_Italy "Propaganda of Fascist Italy") such as *[Scipione l'africano](/wiki/Scipione_l%27africano "Scipione l'africano")* (*Scipio Africanus: The Defeat of Hannibal*, 1937) and *[Luciano Serra, Pilot](/wiki/Luciano_Serra,_Pilot "Luciano Serra, Pilot")* (1938) as well as more straightforward entertainment films.

Arata worked with [Roberto Rossellini](/wiki/Roberto_Rossellini "Roberto Rossellini") on the 1945 [neorealist](/wiki/Italian_neorealism "Italian neorealism") drama *[Rome, Open City](/wiki/Rome,_Open_City "Rome, Open City")*. He was instrumental in securing the backing of the [distribution](/wiki/Film_distribution "Film distribution") company [Minerva Film](/wiki/Minerva_Film "Minerva Film") for the production's release.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Following the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), Arata worked on several [co-productions](/wiki/Co-production_(filmmaking) "Co-production (filmmaking)") with Britain and the United States.
